note: The DreamLayer plugin registry. Community-contributed plugins, validated by CI before they land. The website, phone app, and Mac panel all read this file. First-party plugins are published by the DreamLayer team (author "DreamLayer Team", official=true). Every plugin declares an api version and a pricing model (all free today; the paid seam is reserved). Each plugin ships its own detail copy (long, forwho) and a screenshot URL; social numbers (downloads/rating/comments) come from the social API when configured.

plugins.name: face-synth
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Your head is the MIDI controller. Tilt to pick the note, tap to play it, and how hard you move is how hard it hits. Get a few people on the mesh and you're a band.
plugins.forwho: For musicians, performers, and anyone who wants to play the world.
long: No controller, no hands, no cables. Where you look is the note, and how hard you move is how hard it hits.
You can't play a wrong note. Everything is locked to a scale, so every twitch of your head lands in key.
Everyone in a GhostMode circle is a separate voice in one band. The notes ride the mesh, so every rig hears the whole thing.

plugins.name: open-food-facts
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Look at a shelf, get the better pick. Nutri-Score becomes a rating, allergens get flagged, straight from open data. No account, no key.
plugins.forwho: For anyone with a diet, an allergy, or a habit of squinting at the small print.
long: This is what gives TasteLens something to rank on. Look at a shelf or a menu and it sorts the options by your rules.
Every item scored A to E by Nutri-Score, allergens flagged, pulled live from the Open Food Facts community database. Free, no API key.
Your diet stays on your device. The only thing that leaves is the product name, so it can look the item up.

plugins.name: currency-converter
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Look at a foreign price and see it in your own money, right on the panel. Live rate, no mental math, no phone.
plugins.forwho: For travellers and anyone shopping across a border.
long: Abroad, prices stop being abstract. Look at a tag or a menu and the number shows up in your home currency, inline.
It uses live exchange rates, free and no account, and shows you the rate it used so you know what you're really paying.
Only the number and its currency get looked up. Never where you are, never what you bought.

plugins.name: hud-reactions
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Throw 🎉 👏 ❤️ 🔥 onto your HUD, and everyone in your GhostMode circle sees it land too.
plugins.forwho: For friends, gigs, and anyone sharing a moment on the mesh.
long: Tap to react. A burst lands on your HUD, and if you're in a circle it lands on theirs. A silent yes across a room.
Only the tiny symbol crosses the mesh, signed and small. Never who you are, never where you're standing.
Made for concerts, watch parties, and inside jokes with the people next to you.

plugins.name: filler-word-counter
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: A quiet coach that counts your um, uh, and you know as you talk, in the corner of your eye.
plugins.forwho: For anyone who presents, pitches, or records.
long: Public speaking, on your side. It listens to your own words and keeps a running count in the corner of your eye, so you hear yourself and drop them.
It knows the usual suspects, um, uh, like, you know, basically, and tracks a rough per-sentence pace as you go.
Nothing is recorded and nothing is sent. The tally lives on your glasses and resets when you're done.

plugins.name: air-drums
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Play a drum kit in the air. Nod for a kick, flick left for a snare, right for a hat. Real MIDI, any DAW.
long: No sticks, no pads. Nod down for a kick, flick left for a snare, right for a hat, up for a crash. Each one fires a General MIDI drum on channel 10.
Velocity follows how hard you move, so it feels dynamic instead of robotic.
Pairs with Face Synth for a hands-free one-person band.

plugins.name: open-library
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Look at a book, get the story behind it — author, year, subjects and where it sits in the canon, from the Open Library open dataset. No account, no key.
plugins.forwho: For readers, browsers, and anyone who picks a book up and wonders about it.
long: Gives TasteLens something to rank a shelf of books on. Look at a cover and it pulls the title, author, first-published year, and subjects.
Straight from the Open Library community dataset — free, no API key, no account.
Your reading stays on your device. The only thing that leaves is the title, so it can look the book up.

plugins.name: vinyl-oracle
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Point at a record sleeve and get the pressing, the year, and what the crate is worth — matched against Discogs. Bring your own token; the crate follows you across sessions.
plugins.forwho: For crate-diggers, collectors, and anyone holding a record they can't quite place.
long: Look at a record sleeve and the Vinyl Oracle names the pressing, the release year, and the going rate.
Matched live against the Discogs database. Add your own Discogs personal-access token once and it's remembered across sessions.
Only the record's identifying text leaves the device to do the lookup — the crate itself stays yours.

plugins.name: pokemon-price
plugins.author: DreamLayer Team
plugins.description: Point at a Pokémon card and see what it's worth — set, number, rarity, and the live market price with a low–high band, matched against the Pokémon TCG API. Bring your own free key for heavy use; the price desk follows you across sessions.
plugins.forwho: For collectors, sorters, and anyone holding a card they can't quite value.
long: Look at a Pokémon card and the panel names the printing and tells you the going rate — the market price with a low–high band.
Matched live against the Pokémon TCG API (pokemontcg.io). Add your own free API key once for heavy use and it's remembered across sessions.
Only the card's printed name leaves the device to do the lookup — the card, and that it's yours, stays yours.
